nativeRange.habitat: Found in shallow reef areas and over rocky substrates across circumglobal tropical waters, typically at depths of 3 to 35 metres, though the species has been recorded as deep as 300 metres.

diet.notes: In the wild, the Glass Eye Squirrelfish feeds on octopuses, pelagic shrimp, mantis shrimp (stomatopods), crabs, small fish, and polychaete worms. In captivity, it has been documented accepting a variety of meaty foods including shrimp, squid, clams, and small fish, and generally transitions to frozen meaty fare once acclimated.

summary: The Glass Eye Squirrelfish (Heteropriacanthus cruentatus) is a large, nocturnal reef fish found across tropical oceans worldwide. It tends to be peaceful toward similarly sized tankmates but will consume small invertebrates and fish, and is generally kept singly with plenty of rockwork for shelter.
careNotes: The Glass Eye Squirrelfish is a nocturnal species that generally hides during daylight hours and becomes active at dusk and into the night. Multiple individuals can be housed together in a larger aquarium when introduced at the same time, which tends to reduce aggression between them. A well-sealed lid is generally advised, as squirrelfish have been known to jump. Care is warranted when handling the fish, as it possesses sharp spines along the gill cover (the operculum) that can cause injury. The species has been reported to consume serpent stars, snails, fireworms, and small crustaceans, so tankmate selection should be made with this in mind. Juveniles display a brown-to-red mottled pattern; adults tend to develop a brighter, more uniform red coloration.
